title: podman search with wildcards fails
body:
### Issue Description

podman search with wildcards fails.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

`podman search registry.opensuse.org/opensuse/*busybox*`


### Describe the results you received

Nothing.

Also the podman e2e tests failed with `podman search registry.access.redhat.com/*openshift*`

https://openqa.opensuse.org/tests/5385965/file/podman_e2e-localintegration.txt

```

Podman search
```

The list of images.

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

OS: Latest openSUSE Tumbleweed

Also fails on Fedora 42.

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
I'm having trouble diagnosing an issue. A `wget` request to the unauthenticated endpoint `registry.access.redhat.com` for `*openshift*` returns zero results, which seems incorrect:

```
wget "https://registry.access.redhat.com/v1/search?n=25&q=%2Aopenshift%2A"

{"num_results":0,"query":"*openshift*","results":[]}
```

A reasonable fix is to implement a mock repository for testing, and then remove the repository component from the scope.

> I have not tested `registry.opensuse.org/opensuse/*busybox*`.

Seems like I get some results again

```
$ podman search registry.access.redhat.com/*openshift*
NAME                                                                    DESCRIPTION
registry.access.redhat.com/openshift3/mysql-55-rhel7                    MySQL 5.5 SQL database server
registry.access.redhat.com/openjdk-tech-preview/openjdk-21-jlink-rhel9  rhcc_registry.access.redhat.com_openjdk-tech...
```

So I guess upstream tests should work again though I think we really need to reconsider how we do these search tests urgently as I mentioned on our dev channel (#podman-dev:matrix.org in case you want to join there @ricardobranco777)

Having an external dependencies on registry content we do not control is always broken and just waiting for problems. The registry side can change or if you try to run the tests without internet they fail as well. Ideally we mock the server part locally in the test so we can guarantee a proper return result all the time.
The thing here is search can either call the v1 or v2 endpoint and AFIAK they really are not standardized so they can also just not work/return no or invalid content, etc... That is why mocking is the only sane solution so we can even be sure to test both parts. It would also fix the flakes we are seeing when the registry is down.

From `podman --log-level debug search registry.opensuse.org/opensuse/*busybox*`

```
DEBU[0000] trying to talk to v1 search endpoint         
DEBU[0000] GET https://registry.opensuse.org/v2/        
DEBU[0001] Ping https://registry.opensuse.org/v2/ status 200 
DEBU[0001] GET https://registry.opensuse.org/v1/search?n=25&q=opensuse%2F%2Abusybox%2A 
DEBU[0001] error getting search results from v1 endpoint "registry.opensuse.org": received unexpected HTTP status: 404 Not Found 
DEBU[0001] trying to talk to v2 search endpoint         
DEBU[0001] GET https://registry.opensuse.org/v2/_catalog 
```

And it seems guess is the v2 implementation doesn't support wildcards at all from looking at the c/image code. v2 is literally listing the entire catalog and the just checking on the client side with strings.Contains against the provided intput and thus the wilcard would not match at all. In general filtering such things on the client side seems extremely inefficient and I guess not something we should ever recommend anyone using.

There doesn't seem to be any docs in the man about that wildcard are expected to be supported at all so it is not clear to me the v2 code should support that. cc @mtrmac
While attempting to implement a test with a mocked registry, I discovered that our `quay.io/libpod/registry:2.8.2` image does not appear to support the `v1` API.

yeah I think we should not use another local registry, to fully mock both endpoints it is best to spin up our own webserver that serves the right content. In test/e2e that should be simply using the go std lib, i.e. I did something like this here https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/e00dfa606a79a6cb304492f2be17b46cd80ddd7e although I didn't have to generate a valid response there.

Yes, this is all … unsatisfactory, shall we say?

The distribution spec (for `/v2/` registries, and historically `github.com/docker/distribution`) only provides the “list repos” API which just doesn’t scale.

The `/v1/` search is the only leftover from an older protocol, `github.com/docker/docker-registry`, so old that I don’t remember ever hearing about a deployment; the search term is, unmodified, passed to the server (and thus the interpretation is entirely up to the server — and reading https://github.com/docker-archive/docker-registry/blob/f93b432d3fc7befa508ab27a590e6d0f78c86401/docker_registry/lib/index/db.py#L156 I think _that_ implementation accepted raw SQL LIKE syntax, not traditional wildcards). But it lets the server deal with scaling, so some registries implement that one (notably Docker Hub).

The Podman documentation already contains various disclaimers:
> Note that **podman search** is not a reliable way to determine the presence or existence of an image.
The search behavior of the v1 and v2 Docker distribution API is specific to the implementation of each registry.
Some registries may not support searching at all.
Further note that searching without a search term only works for registries that implement the v2 API.

(My personal opinion is that no-one should ever use `podman search` for ~any purpose, just like one wouldn’t ever install a software package by typing a subset of the name into a search engine and installing the first result, right? … right? Especially in enterprise contexts where support lifetimes, EOLs and vulnerability fix policies matter. So I’m interested in keeping the existing code working, but I’m not strongly motivated to work on trying to fix the overall state of search in the ecosystem.)
